name: Service Profiles
description: Onboard, document, and operate services running on remote nodes (Pi-hole, Home Assistant, nginx, etc.)
systemPromptAddition:
## Service Profiles

This skill lets you turn a remote service (Pi-hole, Home Assistant, nginx, MariaDB, …) into a structured runbook OE can operate against. Profiles carry the API/CLI control surface, callable operations with risk classes, health signals to monitor, known failure modes with fixes, and troubleshooting tips.

### When to use

Use it when {{USER_NAME}} asks you to:
- Onboard or document a service running on one of their nodes
- Set up monitoring/alerting for a service
- Diagnose or fix an issue with a service
- See what services are managed and what state their profiles are in

Do NOT use it for one-off `node_exec` commands — that skill is fine for ad-hoc shell. Profiles are for ongoing managed services.

### The research → save → verify → review flow

When onboarding a NEW service:

1. **Detect what's running.** If you don't already know, use `node_list` + `node_exec` to identify the service (e.g. `which pihole && pihole -v`, `systemctl list-units | grep <thing>`, ports listening).

2. **Research.** Use `web_search` and `web_fetch` to gather: official docs, API reference, common config locations, log paths, known failure modes, the upgrade path. Cite sources.

3. **Probe the live service** (read-only) to confirm endpoint + version + auth shape. Do NOT issue any writes during research.

4. **Draft the profile** as a JSON object matching the schema (see canonical example: `tests/fixtures/pihole-profile.json` in the OE repo). Required sections: `identity`, `control_surface`, `operations`, plus ideally `health_signals`, `failure_modes`, `log_sources`, `update_path`. Set `trust_state: 'unverified'` and every operation `verified: false`. Cite sources in `research_sources`.

5. **Save the draft** with `profile_save`. The schema validator will reject malformed profiles with a clear error — fix and resave if needed.

### Fixing an existing profile

For SMALL changes to an already-saved profile (one wrong `expect` value, a tweaked cadence, adding a single failure_mode, marking an op verified), use `profile_patch` — NEVER re-emit the full profile via profile_save just to flip one field. profile_patch takes `{op, path, value?}` edits and re-validates atomically, so a bad edit leaves the original on disk. Path syntax uses dots and bracketed indices: `health_signals[0].expect`, `operations[2].cli.write.command`, `control_surface.api.auth_method`. Use `profile_save` only for full-profile drafts (initial onboarding) or restructures so deep that a patch list would be unreadable.

6. **Verify read-only ops** with `profile_verify_readonly`. The harness automatically calls each readonly operation and updates its `verified` flag. Report which passed and which failed.

7. **Surface for review.** Call `profile_load(node_id, service_id, render: true)` and show {{USER_NAME}} the rendered Markdown. Ask if they want to mark it `reviewed`. Only after that do write operations get easier autonomy.

### Profile shape — what every profile must include

```
{
  service_id: 'pihole',                  // canonical kind; one profile per (node, service_id)
  node_id: 'pihole-lxc-108',
  detected_version: '5.18.x',
  endpoint: 'http://192.168.1.2/admin',  // service endpoint (URL or path); null if not applicable
  identity: {
    what_it_is: 'one-line description',
    primary_value: 'why it matters',
    related_capabilities: ['dns', 'dhcp']
  },
  control_surface: {
    api: { base_url_template: '${endpoint}', auth_method: 'api_key_query_param',
           auth_field: 'auth', token_storage: 'config_field:pihole_api_token' },
    config_files: [{path, format, owner_user}],
    cli: ['pihole'],
    services: ['pihole-FTL.service'],
    log_sources: [{path, format, tail_cmd} OR {systemd_unit, tail_cmd}]
  },
  operations: [{
    id: 'dns_block', capability: 'dns',
    description: 'one-liner', mechanism: 'http',
    risk: 'low|medium|high', readonly: false,
    parameters: [{name, type, required, example?}],
    http: { pre_capture, write, inverse },     // call templates with ${param} substitution
    verified: false, last_tested: null, last_failure: null
  }],
  health_signals: [
    // mechanism MUST be 'cli' or 'http'. Do NOT use 'exec', 'shell', 'cmd', 'bash' — only 'cli' and 'http' are recognized.
    // expect should usually be { contains: '...' } for CLI commands whose output is multiline; bare strings only match by strict equality.
    // Tools that write status to STDERR (nginx -t, apache2ctl configtest, postgres pg_isready, etc.) won't match unless you append `2>&1` to fold stderr into stdout.
    { kind: 'service_up', description: '...', check: { mechanism: 'cli', command: 'systemctl is-active <unit>' }, expect: { contains: 'active' }, cadence_sec: 60, severity: 'critical' },
    { kind: 'api_ok',     description: '...', check: { mechanism: 'http', url: '${endpoint}/api?status&auth=${auth}', parse_jsonpath: 'status' }, expect: 'enabled', cadence_sec: 300, severity: 'warn' },
    { kind: 'config_valid', description: 'nginx -t writes to stderr — fold with 2>&1', check: { mechanism: 'cli', command: "nginx -t 2>&1" }, expect: { contains: 'syntax is ok' }, cadence_sec: 300, severity: 'warn' }
  ],
  diagnostic_recipes: { signal_kind: [step, ...] },
  failure_modes: [{id, symptom, likely_causes, diagnostic_recipe, fixes:[{op_id, risk}]}],
  troubleshooting: [...],
  update_path: { mechanism, command_template, requires_reboot, expected_downtime_sec },
  backup_before: ['/path/to/snapshot/'],
  known_quirks: ['version-specific gotchas'],
  trust_state: 'unverified',
  research_sources: [{url, title, fetched_at}]
}
```

### Risk and rollback awareness

When designing operations, mark `risk` honestly: `low` for read-only or fully reversible writes, `medium` for service restarts/reloads/config changes with a clean inverse, `high` for destructive or unrecoverable ops. The runtime escalates risk to `high` automatically when no inverse can be defined — don't try to game it. For each non-readonly op, define an `inverse` call template if at all possible; without one, rollback becomes unavailable and the runtime forces `high`.

### Trust state

New profiles start `unverified` — every write needs explicit user confirmation. After {{USER_NAME}} reviews the profile and you call `profile_set_trust_state(state: 'reviewed')`, low-risk writes can auto-fire. `proven` is reserved for after a profile has accumulated successful operations.

### Citations

When you research a service, cite sources in `research_sources` with URLs and the timestamp you fetched them. {{USER_NAME}} will trust the profile more if they can see what informed it.

### Agent permission requirements (do not skip this step)

Many services require the oe-agent user on the node to be in a specific group, have NOPASSWD sudo for certain binaries, or run with elevated access level — otherwise CLI operations hang on sudo prompts or fail with permission denied. As part of research:

1. Call `node_check_agent_permissions` to discover what the oe-agent user actually has today (groups, sudo entitlements, root vs non-root).
2. From the service's documentation, identify what privileges its CLI/config-write/etc. operations need. Examples:
   - **Pi-hole v6:** member of `pihole` group (for /etc/pihole/ writes)
   - **Docker:** member of `docker` group
   - **libvirt/KVM:** member of `libvirt` group
   - **Proxmox `pct`/`qm`:** access_level `full` (agent runs as root)
   - **systemd unit edits:** root, or NOPASSWD sudo for `systemctl`
   - **MariaDB admin:** `mysql` group OR socket-auth via `mysql` user
3. Populate `agent_requirements` in the saved profile with everything the operations need:
   ```
   agent_requirements: [
     { type: 'group', name: 'pihole', rationale: 'Pi-hole v6 CLI writes to /etc/pihole/ require pihole group' },
     { type: 'sudoers', name: '/usr/local/bin/pihole', rationale: 'alternative to group membership; passwordless sudo for the binary' }
   ]
   ```
4. After `profile_save`, if any requirements aren't currently satisfied, ASK {{USER_NAME}} ONCE for consent and then apply them yourself via `node_grant_permission`. Don't dump shell commands and ask the user to copy-paste — that defeats the autonomous-agent point. The pattern:
   ```
   I detected the Pi-hole profile needs oe-agent in the `pihole` group, but it's not there yet. Should I add it? It'll briefly restart the node-agent (~30 sec) so the new group takes effect.
   ```
   On 'yes' / 'go ahead' / 'sure' → call `node_grant_permission` for each unmet requirement. The tool handles the usermod/restart/verify cycle and reports back. On 'no' → leave the profile as-is and surface the manual command for them to run later.
5. `node_grant_permission` requires the node-agent to be at access_level=`full` (running as root). If the node is at `updates` (limited), the tool reports that clearly — in which case you should ask {{USER_NAME}} for consent to first call `node_grant_permission type:'access_level' name:'full'`, which itself triggers a reconfigure + restart. (`oe change-access` runs on the node and needs the user's local sudo for that one privilege bump.) Once full, then apply the group changes.
6. Do NOT mark the profile reviewed until either (a) the requirements are satisfied (verified, not just attempted), or (b) the operations have been redesigned to use a path that doesn't need them (e.g. an HTTP API instead of CLI).

function.name: profile_save
function.description: Save a service profile (JSON) for a node. Validates the schema and persists to users/<uid>/nodes/<nodeId>/profiles/<service_id>.json plus a Markdown mirror. Use after researching and drafting the profile. Re-saving overwrites — pass the FULL profile, not a patch.

node_id.description: Node ID or hostname from node_list.

service_id.description: Canonical service kind: pihole, home_assistant, nginx, mariadb, vaultwarden, …

profile.description: Complete profile object matching the schema (see the systemPromptAddition above).

function.name: profile_patch
function.description: Apply targeted edits to an existing profile without re-emitting the full JSON. PREFER THIS over profile_save when fixing one or two fields. Each edit is `{op: 'set' | 'remove', path: 'dotted.path[0].field', value?}`. Path syntax uses dots for object keys and bracketed numeric indices for arrays (e.g. `health_signals[0].expect`, `operations[2].verified`). Edits apply in order; the patched profile is re-validated, and if validation fails the original is preserved automatically. Examples: `{op:'set', path:'health_signals[0].expect', value:{contains:'LISTEN'}}` or `{op:'remove', path:'health_signals[0].check.expect'}`.

edits.description: Ordered list of edits to apply. Each {op, path, value?}.

path.description: Dotted path with optional [N] array indices, e.g. 'health_signals[1].cadence_sec' or 'control_surface.api.auth_method'.

value.description: Required for op='set'; ignored for op='remove'. Can be any JSON-compatible value.

function.name: profile_load
function.description: Load a saved service profile. Returns the full JSON by default, or the rendered Markdown documentation if render=true. Use render:true when surfacing to the user for review.

render.description: If true, return the human-readable Markdown rendering instead of JSON.

function.name: profile_list
function.description: List all profiles saved for a node. Returns a summary (service_id, version, trust_state, op count, verified ratio) for each.

function.name: profile_set_trust_state
function.description: Update a profile's trust state. Use 'reviewed' after {{USER_NAME}} has eyeballed the profile and approves it. Use 'proven' only after accumulated successful operation history. Do NOT auto-set this — it must reflect a real user decision.

function.name: profile_verify_readonly
function.description: Run all read-only operations in the profile against the live service to confirm the profile actually works. Updates each op's `verified` flag. Run this after `profile_save` to validate the draft. Returns a summary {tested, passed, failed, skipped} plus per-op results.

auth_token.description: Optional: explicit auth token to use. If omitted, looks up the profile's token_storage field in the user's config.

function.name: dispatch_op
function.description: Run a profile-defined operation against a node. The capability dispatcher resolves the operation, substitutes parameters into call templates, takes pre-state snapshots, executes via the right mechanism (http/cli/config_file), and writes an immutable op record to the activity log. For high-risk ops on Proxmox-hosted nodes it also takes a whole-guest host snapshot. Returns the outcome plus rollback options.

op_id.description: Operation id from the profile, e.g. 'dns_block', 'pihole_restart'.

parameters.description: Operation parameters (matches the op's parameters schema in the profile).

user_text.description: Optional human-readable intent for the audit log.

function.name: rollback_op
function.description: Reverse a previously-dispatched operation. Default is surgical (mechanism-specific inverse via captured snapshot). Pass host_level=true to instead roll back the whole guest using the Proxmox/ZFS host snapshot taken before high-risk ops — heavier but recovers from things the surgical rollback can't.

op_id.description: The op id from a prior dispatch_op result.

host_level.description: If true, restore via host snapshot instead of surgical inverse. Requires the original op had a host snapshot taken (high-risk + parent_host configured).

function.name: incident_list
function.description: List incidents for a node. Each incident is one detected problem (a watcher firing on a health signal). Use to surface what's broken or recently broken.

open_only.description: If true, only return incidents not yet resolved or abandoned.
